games.explanation: Bradish has been carving up right-handed bats in his small sample back from injury, which would matter more if the Yankees weren't planning to send a left-handed wall at him. The splits flip hard the other way, with lefties posting a .306 xwOBA and walking at a 12.3% clip against him, and New York happens to roll out Grisham, Rice, Judge and Bellinger in a row. Weathers isn't a star but he doesn't need to be against a Baltimore lineup that's been getting by on contact rather than damage. The market is pricing the name on the mound, not the handedness chart behind him, and -160 is the discount for reading one column further.

Spencer Arrighetti has a walk problem that the market keeps politely ignoring, and Boston brought exactly the lineup to exploit it. He's issuing free passes to nearly 16% of the hitters he faces, and the lefty bats he'll see tonight, Duran and Anthony and Abreu, are the ones tagging him for a .384 xwOBA. Connelly Early counters with a strikeout rate north of 35% and the kind of command Arrighetti can only dream about, which is the entire game in one sentence. A cold night with the wind blowing in turns this into a low-scoring fight, and low-scoring fights belong to the team that isn't walking the ballpark. -124 on the better arm and the better matchup is the cleanest part of the card.

The market is treating this like a coin flip, which is generous to a Giants club starting a pitcher who can't get lefties out and isn't all that scary against righties either. Tampa happens to be built to exploit exactly that hole, with Aranda posting a .410 xwOBA against right-handers and Caminero hitting the ball harder than just about anyone in this lineup. Roupp's walk problem against lefties (12 percent) turns into free baserunners in front of the bats that actually do damage, and Griffin Jax on the other side is the kind of strikeout arm that quietly steals close games. Tampa is the better team by a clear margin, and -108 is asking you to pay nothing for that gap.
